intro: March 2001. The dot-com bubble just went boom. Amazon's stock speedran from $100+ to single digits, Barron's put 'Amazon.bomb' on the cover (savage), and analysts are drawing up your bankruptcy for fun. You've never made a dollar of profit. You run this thing.

realOutcome.summary: Amazon tanked and then became one of the most valuable companies alive. Every 'what actually happened' below is real, no cap.

decisions.scenario: February 2000. The market is euphoric but your spidey-sense says top. You can raise $672M in convertible bonds RIGHT NOW — pricey, dilutive, and it looks paranoid while everyone's still popping bottles.

choices.text: Grab the $672M before the door slams. Secure the bag.
choices.reveal: Bezos raised $672M in convertible notes WEEKS before the bubble popped. That war chest is the entire reason Amazon didn't die in 2001 when funding froze into a brick. Cracked timing.

choices.text: Stay lean, raising cash looks weak
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: The funding window shuts in months and never reopens for unprofitable dot-coms. You run dry in 2001 next to Pets.com and Webvan in the group chat of the damned.

choices.text: Sell the whole company while the stock's still cooking
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You cash out at the top and Amazon gets absorbed into some retailer that never builds AWS or Prime. W trade, historic L for humanity.

decisions.scenario: Your own retail buyers are BEGGING you not to, but you could open your product pages to third-party sellers — including rivals — letting them post up right next to your own stuff.

choices.text: Open Marketplace. Let the competition sell on your turf.
choices.reveal: Amazon opened Marketplace to third parties in 2000-2001. Felt like self-sabotage; became half of all units sold and a fat high-margin machine. Big brain.

choices.text: First-party only, control the whole vibe
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You stay a pure retailer eating every gram of inventory risk. eBay runs third-party commerce and Amazon grows at a fraction of the speed. Mid.

choices.text: Franchise the Amazon brand out to other stores
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You license your name and fumble the customer experience that was your ONLY moat. The brand dilutes into background noise.

decisions.scenario: Wall Street wants receipts that you can EVER make money. You can grind toward your first-ever profit and get obsessed with free cash flow — or keep dumping everything into growth and dare them to doubt you.

choices.text: Grind to first profit and make free cash flow your religion.
choices.reveal: Amazon posted its first-ever profit in Q4 2001 — a skinny $5M — and Bezos rebuilt the whole company around free cash flow per share. Bought years of 'trust me bro' credibility.

choices.text: Profit is for losers, spend for pure growth
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Market's in ashes, no profit in sight, and lenders ghost you. Growth you can't finance is just a bankruptcy with extra steps.

choices.text: Force profit overnight with brutal layoffs
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You hit a number by gutting the company, then can't fund the infrastructure that becomes Prime and AWS. You optimized the wrong decade, king.

decisions.scenario: Your engineering org is spaghetti. You could mandate that every single team expose its work as a service through APIs — a huge painful re-architecture with no obvious payoff — or just let the machine keep running.

choices.text: Mandate everything as APIs. Services or you're fired.
choices.reveal: Bezos' legendary ~2002 'API mandate' forced every team to build service interfaces (rumored ending: 'anyone who doesn't will be fired'). That's the seed that made AWS exist. Absolute foresight.

choices.text: Keep the monolith, put every dev on retail
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Retail ships a little faster this quarter, but the AWS plumbing never gets laid. You fumble the highest-margin business you'd ever build.

choices.text: Infrastructure is a cost center, minimize it
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You starve the systems that could've been a cloud empire. Somebody else invents the cloud and rents it right back to you. Painful.

decisions.scenario: You want to offer unlimited two-day shipping for a flat $79/year. Finance is FREAKING OUT — the math looks like arson, and the heavy shippers will farm it.

choices.text: Launch Prime at $79. Finance can cope.
choices.reveal: Amazon launched Prime in February 2005 while the finance team was internally screaming. It became the most effective loyalty program in retail history — the reason you now buy literally everything from them.

choices.text: Keep per-order shipping fees, that's real margin
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You protect shipping revenue and never build the habit loop that makes Amazon the default. Customers keep comparison-shopping the whole web. Fumbled the addiction.

choices.text: Just raise the free-shipping order threshold
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A safe little tweak that changes nothing structural. The membership flywheel that rewired human shopping behavior never spins up.

bands.line: You turned the crash into a launchpad. Legend episode: WON. No notes.
Alive and profitable, but no cloud, no Prime flywheel. A solid store, not an empire. Mid.

endings.ctaLine: Amazon 300x'd after this fork. Today's agents flagged a company written off at a similar low →
